points: Governance is the process of making decisions, organizing society with rules, and ensuring compliance; it is essential because large societies cannot function without rules and order. Government is the institutional mechanism that makes and enforces these rules through three distinct organs: Legislature (makes laws), Executive (implements laws), and Judiciary (interprets laws and ensures justice). The separation of powers and system of checks and balances ensures that no single branch becomes too powerful and that government remains accountable to the people.

Government operates at three levels: Local (village/town level for immediate community needs), State (regional level for state-wide issues), and Central/National (for national issues and interstate matters). This multi-level structure follows the principle of subsidiarity — problems are addressed at the most appropriate scale where they can be most efficiently solved. India has this three-level governmental structure at all three tiers, with the legislature making laws, executive implementing them, and judiciary ensuring justice.

Democracy is a system where power ultimately rests with the people. In large modern societies, representative democracy is used where citizens vote for elected representatives (MLAs at state, MPs at national level) who make decisions on their behalf. Grassroots democracy refers to citizen participation in decisions at the community level. Strong democracy requires both representative structures (at state and national levels) and grassroots participation (at local levels), ensuring that power flows both top-down and bottom-up. India is a representative democracy with 970+ million voters, and its institutions are rooted in ancient philosophical values about truth, justice, and dharma (righteousness).
